questions.question: What happens when you call `List.of("a", "b").add("c")`?
options: Returns a new list with 3 elements
Compilation error — List.of() returns an immutable type
questions.explanation: List.of() returns an unmodifiable list. The add() method compiles because the return type is List<String>, but throws UnsupportedOperationException at runtime.

questions.question: Which Set implementation maintains insertion order?
questions.explanation: LinkedHashSet maintains a doubly-linked list through its entries, preserving the order in which elements were inserted. HashSet has no ordering, TreeSet sorts elements.

questions.question: What is the time complexity of `ArrayList.get(int index)`?
questions.explanation: ArrayList is backed by an array, so random access by index is O(1). This is its primary advantage over LinkedList, which requires O(n) traversal.

questions.question: Which method is new in Java 21's SequencedCollection?
questions.explanation: Java 21 added SequencedCollection with getFirst(), getLast(), addFirst(), addLast(), removeFirst(), removeLast(), and reversed() methods.

questions.question: What does `Set.of("a", "a")` do?
options: Creates a set with one element "a"
Creates a set with two elements
questions.explanation: Unlike HashSet.add() which silently ignores duplicates, Set.of() throws IllegalArgumentException if duplicate elements are provided. This is a fail-fast design to catch mistakes early.
